EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding just west of Mason. Highway 70 was closed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure with associated surface low and cold front moved across Eastern Tennessee late in the afternoon with extension into the early evening hours. This allowed for the formation of scattered convection in the moderately unstable atmospheric conditions just ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Gamble Gap Road two miles northwest of Tellico Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure with associated surface low and cold front moved across Eastern Tennessee late in the afternoon with extension into the early evening hours. This allowed for the formation of scattered convection in the moderately unstable atmospheric conditions just ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down six miles east of Cleveland in the vicinity of Climer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure with associated surface low and cold front moved across Eastern Tennessee late in the afternoon with extension into the early evening hours. This allowed for the formation of scattered convection in the moderately unstable atmospheric conditions just ahead of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 100 year old elm tree fell onto a home which resulted in damage to the home's deck, back room, and the roof over the kitchen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered convection formed mainly north of Interstate 40 across Northeast Tennessee.  Storm formation was initiated by an upper level trough of low pressure crossing the Southern Appalachians in a weakly to moderately unstable air mass in place across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in Kingsport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered convection formed mainly north of Interstate 40 across Northeast Tennessee.  Storm formation was initiated by an upper level trough of low pressure crossing the Southern Appalachians in a weakly to moderately unstable air mass in place across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) composed of a few mini-supercell thunderstorms tracked eastward through southern middle Tennessee.  One such storm produced a brief tornado just south of Fayetteville.  The tornado uprooted numerous large trees and produced minor damage at a residence.  One person sustained a minor injury at their home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the Lynchburg Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) composed of a few mini-supercell thunderstorms tracked eastward through southern middle Tennessee.  One such storm produced a brief tornado just south of Fayetteville.  The tornado uprooted numerous large trees and produced minor damage at a residence. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a house on Buford Hughey Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, shower and thunderstorm activity produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall across Lincoln and Moore counties.  This flooding closed six roads in Lincoln county and many roads across Moore county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at the intersection of state road 110 and Scott Orchard road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, shower and thunderstorm activity produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall across Lincoln and Moore counties.  This flooding closed six roads in Lincoln county and many roads across Moore county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stoneboro road in Belleville was reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to drop into the mid to upper 20s, causing widespread freezing conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to drop to around 30 degrees across Lincoln county.  The temperature was 32 degrees or lower for at least 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to drop into the mid to upper 20s, causing widespread freezing conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to fall into the upper 20s and lower 30s across Moore county. The temperature was 32 degrees or lower for at least 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in and around Henderson. Numerous streets and roads were flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding between Bolivar and Middleton. Highway 18 North near the Hatchie River and Highway 125 South near Hannis Road were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through the region starting on the evening of October 29th and exited the region by late afternoon on October 30th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of northwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of the Reelfoot Lake Campground were flooded. Parts of State Highway 22 were flooded. Several homes in the vicinity had water up to their porches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance which moved through the region produced some thunderstorms during the evening of October 1st and the morning of October 2nd. These thunderstorms produced some gusty winds and quite a bit of lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house was damaged by a fire started by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the region on October 6th producing some thunderstorms over portions of West Tennessee. These thunderstorms produced quite a bit of lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning damaged parts of First Baptist Church in Paris. The church suffered damage to its chimney, roof, phone system and electrical system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the Mid-South during the day on September 20th, 2009. Heavy rain fell in association with the disturbance causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded a low water crossing at the Agricenter International Complex. Water was at least 18 inches high. A car became stranded in the water when the motorist tried to drive through the flood water. Minor flooding occurred in the bottom floor of the Agricenter building as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down six miles west of Embreeville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse convection developed ahead of a frontal boundary which was moving slowly south across the Kentucky border into Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 17th-September 18th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded Lick Creek Road, south of Shiloh National Military Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough instability was present in the vicinity of an upper level low pressure system in place over Eastern Kentucky with aid from a weak surface trough for the generation of isolated severe convection across Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Horton Highway near Jearoldstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough instability was present in the vicinity of an upper level low pressure system in place over Eastern Kentucky with aid from a weak surface trough for the generation of isolated severe convection across Northeast Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced strong winds, which damaged some bleachers at Riceville Youth football field in Riceville, Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from an isolated thunderstorm produced damage to a few bleachers at the Riceville Youth football field in Riceville, Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 17th-September 18th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding around Memphis. Portions of Airways Road was flooded between Holmes and Stateline Roads. Poplar Avenue between Ridgeway and Kirby was flooded as well. The ramp at Interstate 40 and Danny Thomas Boulevard was closed due to flood water as well as many other streets in the city. As much as 3 feet of water stalled vehicles at the intersection of Avalon and Overton. A daycare center had to be evacuated due to flood water at Millbranch and East Shelby Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 17th-September 18th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded creeks along Bible Hill Road. The road was closed due to flood waters as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours, shower and thunderstorm activity produced 2 to 4 inches of rainfall across Lincoln and Moore counties.  This flooding closed six roads in Lincoln county and many roads across Moore county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding caused numerous roads across Moore county, including Highway 129 near Booneville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee during the late evening hours of February 17th, 2009. The thunderstorms developed out ahead of a developing low pressure system located over the Central Plains. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing large hail.

Low-topped supercell thunderstorms developed along a powerful cold front during the late afternoon of the 28th.  The storms evolved into a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) with embedded mesocyclones, one of which became tornadic.  There were also a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado snapped numerous trees and uprooted several cedar tree trunks along a 1.5 mile path north of U.S. highway 64 between Huntland and Maxwell.  Shingles and roof panels of a home and a barn were peeled back along Stratton Road and Pleasant Ridge Road near Branchville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-topped supercell thunderstorms developed along a powerful cold front during the late afternoon of the 28th.  The storms evolved into a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) with embedded mesocyclones, one of which became tornadic.  There were also a few reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down into a house near Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee during the late evening hours of February 17th, 2009. The thunderstorms developed out ahead of a developing low pressure system located over the Central Plains. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing large hail.
Strong winds between 20 to 30 mph affected West Tennessee during the afternoon of February 3, 2009. An 18-wheeler was overturned in Jackson as a result of the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 18-wheeler overturned in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved through Arkansas during the night of February 28th, 2009. The upper level low produced bands of snow across West Tennessee during the evening hours which continued into the early morning hours of March 1st, 2009. Total snow accumulations ranged from a half of an inch to as much as 16 inches. The hardest hit area occurred in Lauderdale, Tipton, Haywood, Madison, Crockett and Fayette Counties where over a foot of snow fell. Roads were hazardous as a result of the snow and many accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to a half of an inch of snow fell across Weakley County during the early morning hours of March 1st. Total snow accumulations for the snowstorm February 28th-March 1st were around 2-3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and strong lift produced by a deepening low pressure system over Georgia moved west into northern Alabama after midnight on March the 1st.  With freezing or below freezing temperatures in place, widespread snowfall began after midnight on the 31st and continued through the morning hours.  Some banding developed just north of Moore county Tennessee producing between 3 and 6 inches in northern portions of Lincoln, Franklin, and Moore counties. Even heavier snowfall amounts between 7 and 10 inches were reported further north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to occasionally heavy snowfall occurred around midnight through the early morning hours. This produced between 3 and 5 inches on grassy surfaces and higher elevations of northern portions of Franklin county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to heavy snowfall began around midnight and continued through the early morning hours in northern and western portions of Lincoln county. This produced snowfall accumulations between 3 and 7 inches on in western and northern portions of the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow was measured on a snowboard at 2.25 inches in two hours in Lewisburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Midsouth during the late evening of April 9, 2009 into the afternoon of April 10, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front during the evening hours and moved into the Midsouth before weakening during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds. By mid-morning, redevelopment of thunderstorms occurred along the front. These thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew trees and power lines down near the jail in Henning.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported by Stewart County Emergency Management Officials just west of Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable air mass developed as early as midday across the central Tennessee Valley.  Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed by midday producing very heavy rainfall and small hail. As instability increased during the mid to late afternoon, more numerous thunderstorms developed.  Outflow boundary collisions triggered rapid development of new storms which became severe rather quickly in a few instances.  A few reports of large hail were received.  Isolated flash flooding occurred in southeastern Lincoln County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in Fayetteville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave affected much of the MidSouth during the period between June 17th and June 28th as an upper ridge of high pressure built over the region. High temperatures soared into the mid to upper 90s with a few days topping out at or just above 100 degrees. Nighttime lows remained in the upper 70s to lower 80s and provided little relief. In addition, high humidity combined with the hot temperatures produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the period. June 23rd was the worst day of the heat wave as heat index values reached over 110 degrees in many locations. A few deaths occurred from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures soared into the mid to upper 90s even reaching 100 degrees or more over over a span of eleven days. The combination of the hot temperatures and high humidity allowed heat indices to reach above 105 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours. Two deaths occurred from the heat.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced roof damage to one home in Mt. Vinson. In addition, one tree fell on another house. Several additional tree limbs and power lines were knocked down in the area.

Showers and thunderstorms developed over Mid-South during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 15th, 2009 due to daytime heating. The storms died out a couple hours after sunset. Some of the storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous winter storm that moved from the southern Plains into the upper Midwest produced a strong pressure gradient across the mid South and Tennessee Valley. Southeast winds of 30 to 40 mph developed during the late afternoon hours and lasted through the evening. By late evening, wind gusts over 50 mph, and in some cases around 60 mph, knocked down numerous trees and power lines.  A squall line of showers associated with a pre-frontal trough further enhanced the damaging winds during the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high pressure brought near or below freezing temperatures to many locations east of interstate 65 in southern middle Tennessee after midnight on the 4th. At the same time, a low pressure moved north along the Atlantic coast, wrapping clouds and precipitation in the form of snow over Franklin county Tennessee. This produced snowfall accumulations bewteen 1 and 2 inches in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snowfall of less than an inch in Lincoln County contributed to numerous traffic accidents.  One accident resulted in 4 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved northeast into southeast Tennessee, producing repeat rainfall over the same area, in particular the South and West Chickamauga Creek basins. The South Chickamauga Creek reached its second highest ever recorded stage at the gage near Chickamauga, TN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall over several hours produced areal flooding from mid morning to late afternoon in Dunlap, Tennessee. A few roads in and around Dunlap, Tennessee had several inches to nearly a foot of water over the road. The vast majority of the flooding was along highway 28 in Sequatchie county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) composed of a few mini-supercell thunderstorms tracked eastward through southern middle Tennessee.  One such storm produced a brief tornado just south of Fayetteville.  The tornado uprooted numerous large trees and produced minor damage at a residence.  One person sustained a minor injury at their home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado with maximum winds of 100 mph struck the south side of Fayetteville, tracking east before lifting shortly beyond Timber Lake.  The most extensive damage occurred in the Timber Lake community between Diemer Road and Timberlake Trail.  Numerous large trees were uprooted, some snapped at their bases.  Roof and minor structural damage occurred at a residence along Timberlake Drive where a male occupant was injured due to a fall while taking shelter.  One person sustained a minor injury at their residence.  Additional trees were uprooted and snapped along Eldad and Grisham Roads east of Timber Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across the county.  Particularly hard hit was the Elora community, where numerous trees were snapped or uprooted. At least one tree fell on a car.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved northeast into southeast Tennessee, producing repeat rainfall over the same area, in particular the South and West Chickamauga Creek basins. The South Chickamauga Creek reached its second highest ever recorded stage at the gage near Chickamauga, TN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor street flooding began, and eventually escalated into areal and river flooding. The South Chickamauga Creek exceeded its banks and flooded surrounding areas of Chattanooga and East Ridge. The West Chickamauga Creek also contributed to flooding along the South Chickamauga, and areas of East Ridge. The South Chickamauga stage reached 28.54', which is the second highest recorded stage on that river. Numerous businesses and roads were affected by the high waters, with several drivers having to be rescued after driving into flooded streets. Creeks flowing off the Cumberland Plateau in northern Hamilton County also caused flooding in the Soddy Daisy areas, closing roads.  One fatality occurred on 9/20/2009, when a 46 year old man, on a wager, tried to swim across a drainage ditch full of rushing water, and was swept into the aqueduct system.  His friend, a 36 year old man, tried to rescue him and sustained minor injuries. Officials report that alcohol was possibly involved. According to officials, the ditch was not out of its banks at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough developed across the central U.S. allowing moisture from the deep south and Gulf of Mexico to move across Middle Tennessee. A strong surface high pressure center was positioned near the Missouri bootheel, ushering in temperatures below freezing across the mid state. The results of the interaction of these two air masses were total snowfall accumulations of one half to almost three inches across locations generally along and west of Interstate 65. Multiple roads across these counties became snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall accumulation of one to two inches was reported across the county by the Fentress County Sheriff's Office. Multiple roads across the county became snow covered, causing hazardous driving conditions and several car accidents. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

He and the female passenger were flown by medical helpicopters from a landing zone at the scene to Huntsvile, Alabama's Hospital, one with serious injuries and one in critical condition. Final condition of both the male driver and female passenger were unkown. A person was injured in a single vehicle rollover accident on Interstate 65 just after 6 AM CST, with the driver being transported from the scene to Hillside Hospital in Pulaski. Final condition of the driver was unkown. Another single vehicle accident occurred at 7:50 AM CST on Highway 64 East near Frankewing. Driver was transported from the scence to Hillside Hospital and later transported to Vanderbilt Hospital by air medical service. Final condition of the driver was unkown. Details on any damage amounts or injuries associated with these accidents were unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced ponding of water on Highway 412 outside of Lexington. A car hydroplaned due to the ponding of water near the intersection of McCall Road. Four occupants of the vehicle was injured as a result of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a line of showers moved across Middle Tennessee from the southwest to the northeast in assocition with an upper level low moving north into the mid Mississippi River Valley during the later afternoon hours, isolated embedded thunderstorms developed within this line of showers resulting in several downed trees and power lines across Hickman County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight hundred Meriwether Lewis Electric Cooperative members had power knocked out beginning at 5:00 PM CST and lasting in some cases until 9:00 AM CST on December 25th as the result of downed trees and power poles. This included a broken power pole in the Pretty Creek area. Also, a transformer that needed repair because of the wind damage was hard to get to becomes several trees had been knocked down, and a bulldozer had to be called in to clear access to it. Residents in Leatherwood, Bucksnort, Pinewood, and Beaverdam communities also were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of abundant moisture, a low pressure system moving through the region, and cold air at the surface caused an ice storm to occur over Northwest Tennessee. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. Toward the end of the event, precipitation changed briefly to sleet and snow. Significant ice accumulations occurred with Lake and Obion Counties being the hardest hit. One to two inches of ice fell across Northwest Tennessee in addition to one inch of snow. A quarter of an inch of ice along with two inches of snow fell across counties stretching from Lauderdale to Carroll. Less than an inch of snow fell across the rest of West Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down from the ice storm. Some counties lost anywhere from seventy to one hundred percent of their power during the peak of the storm. This occurred mainly across extreme Northwest Tennessee. Roads were very hazardous to travel and many accidents occurred as a result. Two deaths occurred from the storm. One death was the result of carbon monoxide poisoning while the other was from the inability to refill an oxygen tank due to the extensive power loss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half inch of ice fell across Tipton County along with one to two inches of snow. Widespread power outages occurred. Roads were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of abundant moisture, a low pressure system moving through the region, and cold air at the surface caused an ice storm to occur over Northwest Tennessee. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. Toward the end of the event, precipitation changed briefly to sleet and snow. Significant ice accumulations occurred with Lake and Obion Counties being the hardest hit. One to two inches of ice fell across Northwest Tennessee in addition to one inch of snow. A quarter of an inch of ice along with two inches of snow fell across counties stretching from Lauderdale to Carroll. Less than an inch of snow fell across the rest of West Tennessee. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down from the ice storm. Some counties lost anywhere from seventy to one hundred percent of their power during the peak of the storm. This occurred mainly across extreme Northwest Tennessee. Roads were very hazardous to travel and many accidents occurred as a result. Two deaths occurred from the storm. One death was the result of carbon monoxide poisoning while the other was from the inability to refill an oxygen tank due to the extensive power loss.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch of ice fell across Obion County. Widespread power outages occurred. Roads were impassable. Three injuries occurred from auto accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across mainly northern portions of the eastern half of Middle Tennessee, flooding/flash flooding occurred throughout the day. Showers developed in association with a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center across the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region that moved slowly east as the day progressed, resulting in copious moisture being ushered across these areas. One of the hardest hit locations was Cookeville in Putnam County which received several inches of rainfall causing multiple road closures, flooded buildings, and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Charlotte Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved through Arkansas during the night of February 28th, 2009. The upper level low produced bands of snow across West Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening hours which continued into the early morning hours of March 1st, 2009. Total snow accumulations ranged from a half of an inch to as much as 16 inches. The hardest hit area occurred in Lauderdale, Tipton, Haywood, Madison, Crockett and Fayette Counties where over a foot of snow fell. Roads were hazardous as a result of the snow and many accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 5 inches of snow fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 28th. Total snow accumulations ending March 1st were around 7 inches in Chester County.

The upper level low produced bands of snow across West Tennessee during the late afternoon and evening hours which continued into the early morning hours of March 1st, 2009. Total snow accumulations ranged from a half of an inch to as much as 16 inches. The hardest hit area occurred in Lauderdale, Tipton, Haywood, Madison, Crockett and Fayette Counties where over a foot of snow fell. Roads were hazardous as a result of the snow and many accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to nine inches of snow fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of February 28th. Total snow accumulations ending March 1st were around 3-12 inches in Shelby County. The hardest hit area was around Bartlett and Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A light freezing rain and drizzle event occurred across portions of the Cumberland Plateau during the late evening hours on the 17th and the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A light drizzle fell on fozen roads resulting in black ice across a wide portion of the county. On Interstate 40, 55 cars and trucks were involved in a chain-reaction crash on a five mile stretch of Interstate 40 between mile marker 329 and 334. In addition, there were over a dozen accidents with injuries scattered across all ends of the county. One women reportedly suffered serious injuries including breaks to both arms. In Crossville, the Cumberland County Chapter of the American Red Cross opened an emergency shelter at the Crab Orchard Church of God of Prophecy and 22 travelers spent the night there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved through Arkansas during the night of February 28th, 2009. The upper level low produced bands of snow across West Tennessee during the evening hours which continued into the early morning hours of March 1st, 2009. Total snow accumulations ranged from a half of an inch to as much as 16 inches. The hardest hit area occurred in Lauderdale, Tipton, Haywood, Madison, Crockett and Fayette Counties where over a foot of snow fell. Roads were hazardous as a result of the snow and many accidents occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An additional 1-2 inches of snow fell across Chester County during the early morning hours of March 1st. Total snow accumulations during the February 28th-March 1st, 2009 snow storm was around 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered low topped supercell thunderstorms developed along a pre-frontal trough during the afternoon hours across middle and eastern Tennessee.  One of these storms tracked east through Franklin County producing isolated wind damage to trees and powerlines.  The storm environment consisted of very strong deep layer wind speed shear and moderate amounts of instability.  On a side note, unseasonably warm temperatures in the 70s were reached during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees onto power line in Winchester.  Large tree limbs were also blown down onto roadways.  Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure center lifted northeast from the southern Plains into the Great Lakes. A tight pressure gradient resulted in strong southerly winds ahead of an associated dry line and pressure trough which pushed eastward through the Tennessee Valley. A broken line of showers and isolated thunderstorms developed along the dry line during the late morning hours, and swept rapidly east across the Tennessee Valley during the early afternoon hours. Although a couple of reports of wind damage occurred with the showers and thunderstorms, the more prominent impact was due to damaging non-thunderstorm winds ahead of and immediately behind the dry line passage. Numerous trees were blown down resulting in sporadic power outages. The roof of a high school gymnasium in Huntland TN was partially torn off as a result of the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sustained period of high winds, including gusts of 60 mph, knocked down several trees and a few power lines.  The most severe damage was at the Huntland High School where a gymnasium was partially torn off.  Power outages were reported in northern Franklin County.

Other minor roof and sign damage occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs and power lines were knocked down county wide due to the strong winds. Minor roof damage occurred as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Midsouth during the late evening of April 9, 2009 into the afternoon of April 10, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front during the evening hours and moved into the Midsouth before weakening during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds. By mid-morning, redevelopment of thunderstorms occurred along the front. These thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a tree down onto a house and ripped the exterior off of a mobile home near Morris Chapel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  The resulting squall line triggered numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports along with large hail.  Three tornadoes were also reported.  One person was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  The resulting squall line triggered numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports along with large hail.  Three tornadoes were also reported.  One person was injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An F2 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 120 mph occurred around 2 miles south of Dunlap.  The tornado initially touched down along Frank Tate Road with a maximum wind speed of 90 mph (EF-1) and a width of 100 yards.  The tornado continued in a northeast path and increased in size to an EF-2 with a maximum wind speed of 120 mph and a width of 175 yards.  Several large hardwood trees were snapped off near the trunk base with extensive damage occurring in a concentrated path.  The tornado continued its northeast movement and finally weakened to a EF-1 with a maximum wind speed of 100 mph as it dissipated at the foothill of Signal Mountain.  A school and several homes suffered minor to moderate wind damage along the nearly 5 mile of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm moving across the Mississipi Valley brought a dry line and cold front into north Alabama during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Several classic supercell thunderstorms developed by early afternoon in northwest Alabama, sweeping across the remainder of north Alabama during mid to late afternoon hours.  Many of the storms produced very large hail, up to baseball and softball sized, producing significant damage, especially from Decatur through Madison and northwest Huntsville.   One of the supercells produced a long track tornado producing damage of up to EF-3 intensity which struck northeastern Marshall County, crossed Lake Guntersville, and moved into southern DeKalb County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A kennel fence was reported blown over. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 Tornado, with maximum estimated wind speed at 100 mph, caused damage that resulted in many large trees that were either snapped or uprooted. Several homes sustained minor roof damage and several outbuildings were also destroyed.||Newspaper reported that the Union Valley Baptist Church received significant roof and exterior wall damage as it was blown off of its foundation. Newspaper also reported that several roads were blocked with debris from buildings and trees and also several roads were closed due to localized flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 Tornado, with maximum estimated wind speed at 100 mph, started 8 miles south-southeast of Hohenwald in Lewis County, or just west of Napier, and moved east into far northwest Lawrence County. It snapped or uprooted many hundreds of large hardwood trees. It also flipped a tractor trailer. The hardest hit area was a large campground in Napier. In addition to the trees, several large camper trailers were damaged or flipped by the tornado. Two people sustained minor injuries and had to be rescued from inside the camper.||Newspaper also reported that three homes received damage but details of damage not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into the Mid-South during the late afternoon into the evening hours of June 3rd, 2009. Some of the thunderstorms became severe and produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast out of the Southern Plains during May 8th into the early morning of May 9th, 2009. Storms associated with the MCS produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in and around Atoka. A home was severely damaged by flooding on Kearns Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that had moved in Middle Tennessee on May 1st moved north as a warm front during the afternoon hours near the Tennessee/Kentucky Border, and in association with a strong low pressure system that had developed across central Arkansas, resulted in flash flooding across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters reached the edge of Highway 641.  Numerous low areas were also flooded, especially near Hatley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south into the Midsouth during the afternoon hours of May 1, 2009. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front that continued into the evening hours. Some storms became severe producing flash flooding and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 8 inches of rain produced flash flooding across much of Carroll County including the city of Huntingdon. Damage to the county road system totaled over $40,000 and 35 percent of the roads has minor to major damage. Multiple roads were closed including Briar Creek Road, Dollar Road, Johnny Cook Road, Maytown Road and Roundhouse Road. Seven culverts were washed out and one bridge embankment was damaged. Other washouts throughout the county needed repair. In the city of Huntingdon a few roads were closed including Carter Road and Third Avenue, South. The parking lot at the Huntingdon Pre-K Headstart was covered by a foot of water. Highway 22 between McKenzie and Huntingdon was partially underwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across Middle Tennessee triggering early morning severe thunderstorms along with a line of severe thunderstorms that moved west to east across the entire mid state from the late morning hours through mid afternoon. This resulted in 10 tornadoes across the mid state, with one being an EF4 Tornado in Murfreesboro in Rutherford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near the city of Flatwoods, hailstones were measured at 3.25 inches and weighed 1/4 pound.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but intense complex of thunderstorms tracked east-southeast clipping far southern middle Tennessee during the late morning and early afternoon of the 10th. Frequent lightning occurred with the strongest storms, sparking a fire that destroyed a cabinet making shop.  A bow echo developed with the cluster of storms as it entered Lincoln County, producing winds of 50 to 60 mph that knocked down numerous trees in southern Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked a tree down onto Chenault Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable air mass developed as early as midday across the central Tennessee Valley.  Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed by midday producing very heavy rainfall and small hail. As instability increased during the mid to late afternoon, more numerous thunderstorms developed.  Outflow boundary collisions triggered rapid development of new storms which became severe rather quickly in a few instances.  A few reports of large hail were received.  Isolated flash flooding occurred in southeastern Lincoln County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported at the intersection of Hwy 231 and the Hwy 64 bypass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Trotwood Avenue closed due to several feet of water covering the road. Additional newspaper reports added that several additional area roads were closed because of the rising water and numerous secondary roads were closed. Police and fire fighters helped several residents from their homes as water poured into basements, especially in neighborhoods near creeks. Several cars were washed away near Brookwood Apartments off West 7th and Academy Lane. Safety officials helped several stranded motorists who were trapped in floodwaters. Several  homes were damaged near Napier Lake Road off the Natchez Trace due to flash flooding. One resident suffered a broken arm and another was cut on the leg, but no major injuries were reported. Columbia Academy's baseball field was flooded and this resulted in the postponement of numerous games, including the District 11-A Baseball Tournament at the Academy. Flash flooding also caused a runoff that produced a hole underneath the|tennis courts at Columbia Academy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that had moved into Middle Tennessee on May 1st moved north as a warm front during the afternoon hours to near the Tennessee/Kentucky Border on May 2nd. It then eventually moved south as a cold front on May 3rd in association with a strong low pressure system that had developed across central Arkansas on May 2nd and moved south into Central Louisiana on May 3rd. This weather pattern resulted in an EF1 tornado, large hail, and flash flooding across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported near City Lake Road near Meadow Park Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that had moved in Middle Tennessee on May 1st moved north as a warm front during the afternoon hours near the Tennessee/Kentucky Border, and in association with a strong low pressure system that had developed across central Arkansas, resulted in flash flooding across several counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that had moved into Middle Tennessee on May 1st moved north as a warm front during the afternoon hours to near the Tennessee/Kentucky Border on May 2nd. It then eventually moved south as a cold front on May 3rd in association with a strong low pressure system that had developed across central Arkansas on May 2nd and moved south into Central Louisiana on May 3rd. This weather pattern resulted in an EF1 tornado, large hail, and flash flooding across several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More than 20 homes stood with 3 to 4 feet of water in them, and crews had to use boats to rescue several people in the Centerville area. Several roads were flooded along with damage to several bridges, especially in the southern portion of the county.|Sulpher, Coble, Brushy, and Cane Creek communities experienced damage as a result of flash flooding. Totally, six homes were flooded, while 29 had water that spread underneath, though not into living areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-tropical low pressure system tracked across the Mid South during the late afternoon and evening of May 24, 2009 resulting in numerous showers and thunderstorms across Western Tennessee. Some of these storms produced heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding. A weak tornado also touched down near the Mississippi River west of Dyersburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down in open farmland near Bradley Road and the Obion River Bridge east of Midway. The tornado tracked northwest across the Great River Road and Highway 104 while lifting and touching down several times. The tornado caused no damage. This tornado crossed the Mississippi River and continued into Pemiscot County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads in the Cane Creek and McCord Hollow area were all but destroyed as flood waters lifted the asphalt from the roadways and piled it up where ever the water receded.  Highway Commissioner Ronnie Darnell said the damage, which was widespread across Lewis County, was the worst he had seen since he took office in 1977.  Bridges across the county also suffered damage as asphalt and dirt around the bridges was washed away. A outbuilding at a sawmill on Slippery Creek Road left its foundation when the flood waters rose, along with other damage at the sawmill directly attributed to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 6,000 customers were without power when a lightning strike struck equipment at a power station in Lebannon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front moved from northern Mississippi northeastward into southwestern portions of Middle Tennesee during the late afternoon hours, the airmass became moist and unstable. Southerly surface flow also developed, and with the influences of daytime heating, produced severe thunderstorms across Lawrence and Wayne Counties. These severe thunderstorms resulted in several reports of large hail and thunderstorm wind damage, along with flash flooding in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down in southeast part of county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into Middle Tennessee from Kentucky, and upper level disturbances moved across the mid state in northwesterly flow aloft, isolated severe thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist unstable airmass that had been present since the late afternoon hours. These thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours across Sumner and Overton Counties. These thunderstorms caused thunderstorm wind damage in both counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down causing power outages in Algood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed along a boundary and tracked across east Tennessee bringing numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.   A few hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The newspaper reported numerous trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds in Jefferson City and countywide.   A few outbuildings were also damaged by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into the northern Cumberland Plateau region of Middle Tennessee , severe thunderstorms developed in a warm and unstable airmass across Overton County during the late afternoon hours, resulting in hail up to the size of golfballs and thunderstorm wind damage in Livingston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into the northern Cumberland Plateau region of Middle Tennessee , severe thunderstorms developed in a warm and unstable airmass across Overton County during the late afternoon hours, resulting in hail up to the size of golfballs and thunderstorm wind damage in Livingston.

One of the reasons was that a tree fell on a substation distribution line crossing North Oak Street owned by the Upper Cumberland Electric Membership Corporation.

An area of showers and thunderstorms developed across northwestern portions of the mid state and moved southeastward. One of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds, producing thunderstorm wind damage in Wilson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were reported down in Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front that was moving east to west across Middle Tennessee transitioned into a cold front by the afternoon hours west of the Tennessee River, and upper level disturbances developed that moved into the mid state from the west, Middle Tennessee was situated for the development of severe thunderstorms as a warm and unstable airmass took hold. Severe thunderstorms did develop during the late morning through late evening hours across mainly northern portions of the mid state, resulting in several thunderstorm wind damage and one hail report being received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baton Rouge Drive in Hermitage experienced penny size hail. Hail just lasted for only a minute.

Other thunderstorms produced lightning that caused major damage to a home in Macon County and an oil well in Overton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning stike directly hit an oil well off Airport Road in Livingston. It caused the entire lid to bend and fly off.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cheatham County Emergency Management Agency Director Edwin Hogan stated that seven homes and six businesses sustained damage. Many trees were downed in the downtown Ashland City area, one at the County Courthouse and one across from the Board of Education. Trees were reported downed on Oak Circle, Cumberland Street, Adkisson Street, Vine Street, and Lenox Street. The steeple at the Ashland City First Baptist Church on Main Street also came down during the storm. Hogan also said that a Cumberland Electric Membership Corporation Pole was down on Frey Street and the roadway was shut down down to traffic. A tower at WQSV-790 AM that supplied the sighnal from the main tower on Chapmansboro Road went down during the storm. The radio was off the air for a short time. A local business had a tree fall on a car in its parking lot and clipped the front porch of the building, moving two of the porch's support beams.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees in Jacks Creek. One storage trailer was knocked over by the winds as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms from an earlier mesoscale convective system that developed over Southeast Kansas and Southwest Missouri moved east-southeast and affected northern parts of the Mid-South. Strong wind shear combined with very unstable air to support storms with damaging winds along and just ahead of a squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked several trees and tree limbs down in Greenfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across the Mid-South during the day on June 12th, 2009. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system developed over Eastern Oklahoma and tracked east into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours. A derecho tracked across the Mid-South producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. Wind speeds were reported as high as 80 mph. Isolated tornadoes occurred along the bow echo. In addition, scattered thunderstorms developed over ahead of the bow echo and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew large trees down in Arlington. Power lines were also knocked down causing power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the roof and started an attic fire in the the Richland Hills Subdivision near Manchester. Exact extent of damage to attic was unkown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm and unstable airmass continued across the Northern Cumberland Plateua region, an area of showers and thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours. One of these thunderstorms reached severe thresholds in Fentress County, producing thunderstorm wind damage. Another thunderstorm that developed in Fentress County produced a lightning stike that destroyed four oil tanks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning strike destroyed an Oil Tank. One of the tanks exploded and flew 300 feet from its original location. Newspaper reported that this one tank broke off a crossmember of a utility pole and severed power and utility lines before coming to rest across the road from its original position. There was only a small amount of oil in the bottom of the tanks, which helped keep a fire that had also broken out due to the lightning strike from spreading to three other tanks in the same area.

Northeast from the church considerable trees were twisted or snapped or uprooted along the damage path along Cliff Park Road, near the intersection of Icy Cove Trail and Arrow Trail and East Cove Road.|Tornado path length was 12 miles with a maximum width of 100 yards.||Newspaper reported that other areas near the Monterey sustained considerable damage, including roofs blown off barns and sheds, trees|uprooted, and fences demolished. In particular, the roof of the tractor|shed, damage to a cattle corral, and miles of fences were demolished, along with a rental house that had its windows shattered, at Senator Charlotte Burks Farm near Monterey.

Based on this damage,   maximum estimated wind speed was around 100 mph, which corresponds to an EF-1 rating on the Enhanced Fujita Scale. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across Middle Tennessee triggering early morning severe thunderstorms along with a line of severe thunderstorms that moved west to east across the entire mid state from the late morning hours through mid afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-4 Tornado...with maximum estimated wind speed around 170 mph, was reached in the Highland Park Drive Neighborhood as well as the|Tomahawk Trace Area. Several well constructed homes were destroyed|in those areas, hardwood trees were debarked, and vehicles were tossed|considerable distances. There were two fatalities, a 30 year old women and her 9 week old daughter, and 58 injuries per newspaper reports, of which at least 7 were serious. Path length was 23.25 miles and maximum width was 750 yards.||Gound surveys of the Murfreesboro tornado indicate that the actual initial touchdown was just north of the Eagleville Community in far|Southwestern Rutherford County. Intermittent tree and roof damage was |noted beginning at a residence near the intersection of Kelly Road and|Highway 41A. Intermittent tree damage continued northeast, with the path becoming continuous along Rocky Grove Road between the Cedar Grove |and Pleasant Hill Communities. The survey team then inspected continuous|damage from Rocky Grove Road northeast to a residence on Newman Road approximately 2 miles north of the Windrow Community. Extensive tree damage was noted along the path...along with multiple instances of roof damage to homes.||The most significant damage was on Patterson Road 1/2 mile west of Windrow. A brick home was completely destroyed and thrown almost|completely off its foundation. ||Additional areas between Stones River Battlefield and Medical Center |Parkway in Murfreesboro were surveyed. Two homes on Wilkinson Pike and two homes on Highland Park Drive were completely destroyed. One of the homes did appear to be properly anchored to it's foundation while three|were not. Per a newspaper report, at Stones River Battlefield, a quarter mile swath of fallen and damaged trees streched one mile across the battlefield, along with numerous fences being destroyed, closing parts of the park to tourists. One particular fallen tree damaged a 60 foot section of the national cemetary wall and a cannon display.||Additional information about the tornado included it tracked due northeast for the first 9 miles then began a steady turn to the right. By the time the tornado lifted it was moving to the east-southeast. Additionally, videos of the tornado indicated a multiple vortex appearance to the tornado and the aerial survey was able to detect focused areas of damage within the larger path.||The aeriel survey revealed 12 to 15 homes which were completely destroyed and dozens others which suffered significant damage. A 2-story office building had the complete upper floor removed.|Numerous large vehicles were picked up and tossed, including|several semi trailers which wound up piled together near the|intersection of Thompson Lane and Broad Street. Significant roof|damage and some wall collapse on homes were also noted.|Several well built homes suffered significant exterior and|interior wall failure. ||Three other areas received significant damage. The first area was along Bushnell Drive just north of Compton Road. A well built 2-storty brick home was almost completely destroyed with just part of the kitchen wall remaining standing. The second area was near the intersection of Haynes Drive and Sulphur Springs Road. Three homes on Cornwall Court were completely destroyed and thrown clear of their slab foundations, however|it did not appear any of the homes was properly anchored.||The third area of enhanced damage was along Battleground Drive between |D`Ann Drive and Tomahawk Trace. Three homes were completely  blown clear of their foundations and destroyed. One home was not |anchored/fastened to the foundation, however, the other 2 were very well constructed. Several trees were also debarked at this location.||Additional information provided by the Rutherford County EMA through a newspaper report stated that over 845 homes were damaged, of these 117 were destroyed, 298 had major damage, 175 had minor damage, and another 255 were affected in some way. 519 Structures were also affected, including 77 destroyed, with an additional 300 plus homes and structures impacted in some way just outside of the city limits. Newspaper also reported an overturned truck caused traffic backup on Interstate 24 and multiple power lines down across the entire city.|Power lines being down resulted in as many as 18,000 homes being without power after the storm.  ||Newspaper also reported that two people were seriously injured and multiple homes were destroyed when the tornado passed through the Wilkinson Pike/Thompson Lane Areas. And that total tonnage associated with the storm gathered at the Rutherford County Landfill was 5,071 tons.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touchdown about 6 miles northeast of Camden at 1041 A.M. CDT. A newspaper reported that the damage began at the Prospect Church and Cemetery, damaging the trim of the church and uprooting trees in the cemetery. It traveled northeast and ended at the Tennessee River at about 1048 A.M. CDT. Damage width was 75 yards wide. Much of the damage was along and near Grapevine and Harmon Dock Roads. Many hundreds of large hardwoood trees and pine trees were snapped or uprooted along the 4 mile damage path. Twelve homes suffered roof and structural damage and several outbuildings, including two barns, were destroyed, two mobile homes were damaged, and at least two cars were damaged per NWS Storm Survey and newspaper reports.  Based on this damage, maximum estimated winds at 100 mph which corresponds to and EF-1 Tornado.||Also...one trailor was destroyed on Harmon Creek Road. Numerous trees were reported down in northern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system moved across Middle Tennessee triggering early morning severe thunderstorms along with a line of severe thunderstorms that moved west to east across the entire mid state from the late morning hours through mid afternoon. This resulted in 10 tornadoes across the mid state, with one being an EF4 Tornado in Murfreesboro in Rutherford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touchdown one half mile north of the intersection of Murfreesboro Road and Locke Creek Road. The tornado then traveled northeast. The tornado damage path was 0.8 miles long and 75 yards wide. The start of the damage was found at the top of the ridge off of Locke Creek Road. Numerous trees were uprooted and twisted. One power pole was snapped. Based on the damage found, winds were estimated at 70 mph which corresponds to an EF-0 Tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado with a maximum wind speed of 135 miles an hour occurred around five miles southeast of Huntsville. The tornado initially touched down just west of the Fairview community with a maximum wind speed of 90 mph (EF-1) and a width of 100 yards. The tornado continued along an east path and increased in size and intensity to an EF-2 with a maximum wind speed at 135 mph and a width of 200 yards. Several large hardwood trees were snapped off near trunk level and a free standing cell phone tower was collapsed and twisted by the tornado winds. Extensive damage occurred in a concentrated path at least 80 yards in length at the 135 mph EF-2 level. The tornado continued east and finally weakened to an EF-1 with a maximum wind speed of 100 mph as it dissipated at the foothill of Gray Mountain. At least seven homes suffered minor to moderate wind damage along the 4.5 mile path of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over Mid-South during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 15th, 2009 due to daytime heating. The storms died out a couple hours after sunset. Some of the storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked trees and power lines down across Gibson County. A roof was blown off of the Hills Trucking Company. Bethel baseball field in McKenzie sustained damage from the winds. Foul poles and flag poles were knocked down in addition to several fences. Windows were blown out of several vehicles parked at the baseball fields as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South during the afternoon hours of July 30th, 2009. A low pressure center developed along the front as the front moved into the area. The low pressure helped to spawn several tornadoes across the Mid-South. In addition, other storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down tree limbs on Interstate 240 at South Parkway in Midtown Memphis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over Mid-South during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 15th, 2009 due to daytime heating. The storms died out a couple hours after sunset. Some of the storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees and power lines in Henderson. Multiple roads were blocked and a few trees fell on vehicles and a home in town creating a hole in a roof. A barn was destroyed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over Mid-South during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 15th, 2009 due to daytime heating. The storms died out a couple hours after sunset. Some of the storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in portions of Decatur County. Bible Hill Road, Cub Creek Road and several other roads were flooded as a result. In addition, a motorist on McIllwain Road had to be rescued from flood waters. People were also trapped in a vehicle while another person was trapped in a home due to flood waters along W O Baker Road. Everyone was rescued safely. In the area of Sugar Tree, three houses and two vehicles were underwater along Mills Road near the Tennessee River. One camper and one boat were also damaged. Five culverts and one bridge were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South during the afternoon hours of July 30th, 2009. A low pressure center developed along the front as the front moved into the area. The low pressure helped to spawn several tornadoes across the Mid-South. In addition, other storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site near Henning, measured an 86 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front moved from northern Mississippi northeastward into southwestern portions of Middle Tennesee during the late afternoon hours, the airmass became moist and unstable. Southerly surface flow also developed, and with the influences of daytime heating, produced severe thunderstorms across Lawrence and Wayne Counties. These severe thunderstorms resulted in several reports of large hail and thunderstorm wind damage, along with flash flooding in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front moved from northern Mississippi northeastward into southwestern portions of Middle Tennesee during the late afternoon hours, the airmass became moist and unstable. Southerly surface flow also developed, and with the influences of daytime heating, produced severe thunderstorms across Lawrence and Wayne Counties. These severe thunderstorms resulted in several reports of large hail and thunderstorm wind damage, along with flash flooding in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were reported blown down, 3 miles west of Highway 43.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS pushed southeast across southern Middle Tennessee. This system downed a few trees in portions of Lincon county and Moore county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down and on fire on Highway 41A near the Bedford, Coffee, and Moore county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS pushed southeast across southern Middle Tennessee. This system downed a few trees in portions of Lincon county and Moore county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln county 911 center reported a tree down on Petersburg Chestnut Ridge road. .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable air mass developed as early as midday across the central Tennessee Valley.  Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed by midday producing very heavy rainfall and small hail. As instability increased during the mid to late afternoon, more numerous thunderstorms developed.  Outflow boundary collisions triggered rapid development of new storms which became severe rather quickly in a few instances.  A few reports of large hail were received.  Isolated flash flooding occurred in southeastern Lincoln County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from strong to severe thunderstorms caused 1 foot of water to accumulate over Echols Road near Vanntown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong thunderstorms dropped southeast through the central Tennessee Valley during the afternoon hours on the 16th.  Due to saturated conditions from previous rainfall and flooding, isolated flash flooding repeated itself from a day before in portions of Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water was reported flowing over Echols Road between Elora and Vanntown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system dropped southeast from the middle Mississippi Valley and into the central Tennessee Valley during the late evening on the 4th.  The line of thunderstorms formed a small scale bow echo over Lincoln County knocking down numerous trees in and around the city of Fayetteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds along and behind a gust front knocked down trees on Boonshill to Petersburg Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system dropped southeast from the middle Mississippi Valley and into the central Tennessee Valley during the late evening on the 4th.  The line of thunderstorms formed a small scale bow echo over Lincoln County knocking down numerous trees in and around the city of Fayetteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds along and behind a gust front knocked down several trees and limbs throughout the city of Fayetteville.  Power outages were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported one tree downed by thunderstorm winds in Luttrell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over several roadways in southwestern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north into portions of West Tennessee on August 1st, 2009 as a cold front pushed in from the west. Low pressure developed along the cold front as it pushed east. Heavy rain developed in advance of the low pressure. Flash flooding occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain fell in 45 minutes in Collierville. Six to eight inches of water covered the intersection of Collierville-Arlington Road and Fletcher Road. The water spilled over the curbs as as result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Iron Hill Road and Interstate 40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave affected much of the MidSouth during the period between June 17th and June 28th as an upper ridge of high pressure built over the region. High temperatures soared into the mid to upper 90s with a few days topping out at or just above 100 degrees. Nighttime lows remained in the upper 70s to lower 80s and provided little relief. In addition, high humidity combined with the hot temperatures produced heat index values of 105 degrees or greater during the period. June 23rd was the worst day of the heat wave as heat index values reached over 110 degrees in many locations. A few deaths occurred from the heat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures soared into the mid to upper 90s even reaching 100 degrees or more over over a span of eleven days. The combination of the hot temperatures and high humidity allowed heat indices to reach above 105 degrees during the afternoon and early evening hours. Seven deaths occurred from the heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South on Independence Day 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 4th. The storms continued into the morning hours of July 5th before dissipating. A few of these storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. Storms redeveloped by the afternoon of July 5th across West Tennessee in the vicinity of the cold front. These storms produced additional flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Millington. One motorist was stranded in flood water at the intersection of Navy Road and Wasp Road. In addition, Arapaho Road was flooded. One child was swept away by flood waters in a drainage ditch at the corner of Sigler Lane and Highway 51. The child was found an hour later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees and power lines in Bolivar and through out the rest of Western Hardeman County. Winds were estimated at 80 mph when the storm passed through Bolivar. One tree fell into a roof of a home in Bolivar causing damage while another landed on a vehicle.

A section of Windle Community Road was left rippled and broken away after the flash flood in that area caused a small creek to swell and flow over the road. A large travel trailer was marked by emergency management officials after flood waters carried it approximately 300 feet from a residence on the other side of East Howard Road. Masters Road had extensive damage near two culverts where flood waters washed the road away. A 25 foot section of Bilbrery Road was missing after being washed out. Sink hole developed|on Thursday, July 16th on Fair Street. Water gushed over the spillway at Livingston City Lake, and the nearby bridge on Old Highway 42 was underwater for a time. A car was submerged under flood waters off West Volunteer Drive as the road collapsed, leading to the rescue of a man and women who were in the car and had to cling to a tree until they could be pulled away from the flooded waters. Neither was seriously injured. Several homes were damaged from flooding. Extent of damage to homes unknown.||Total maximum rainfall amount reported to the National Weather Service Office in Old Hickory was 8.9 inches.

With instability in the atmosphere still present, and lift being provided by approaching frontal dynamics, thunderstorms developed that produced heavy rainfall resulting in flash flooding across four counties in the mid state through the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wayne County Sheriff Office reported several streets in Waynesboro and in the surrounding areas were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong low pressure system and associated front moved quickly from the Midwest U.S. into Middle Tennessee by the afternoon hours, and through the mid state during the evening hours, thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this weather system. This thunderstorm development was supported by southerly, rich in moisture, flow that was well established through the upper levels of the atmosphere. The resulting combination of moisture, instability, and lift caused a variety of severe weather to occur in three different counties of the mid state during the evening hours. Damaging thunderstorm winds in Perry County, flash flooding in Montgomery County, and an EF1 Tornado in Sumner County. The tornado in Sumner County downed trees and power lines along with causing damage to several structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few hundred trees were either snapped off or uprooted. Power lines were snapped also in the area. A few homes sustained very light damage with windows blown off. Some homes had shingles blown off. One large barn was flattened. Several outbuildings and carports were also damaged. Winds were estimated between 95 MPH and 100 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With southerly, rich in moisture, flow well established into the upper level of the atmosphere, and daytime heating influences providing instability and lift, thunderstorms developed across the mid state during the afternoon hours. One of these thunderstorms produced flash flooding in Putnam County. Another thunderstorm spawned a brief small tornado 7 miles east of McMinnville in Warren County. Several outbuildings and a grain silo were destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn, grain silo, and several buildings were destroyed.  A residence suffered minor roof damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 200 households lost power in northern portions of Clarksville when the Edgeton substation was struck by lightning.  Power lines and transformers associated with the substation were damaged also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across mainly northern portions of the eastern half of Middle Tennessee, flooding/flash flooding occurred throughout the day. Showers developed in association with a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center across the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region that moved slowly east as the day progressed, resulting in copious moisture being ushered across these areas. One of the hardest hit locations was Cookeville in Putnam County which received several inches of rainfall causing multiple road closures, flooded buildings, and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inches of water was reported along Cumby Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across mainly northern portions of the eastern half of Middle Tennessee, flooding/flash flooding occurred throughout the day. Showers developed in association with a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center across the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region that moved slowly east as the day progressed, resulting in copious moisture being ushered across these areas. One of the hardest hit locations was Cookeville in Putnam County which received several inches of rainfall causing multiple road closures, flooded buildings, and water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one apartment complex was evacuated in Cookeville where an estimated 6 inches of rain fell. Putnam County's 18 schools were dismissed one hour early so students could get home before streets flooded. Standing water was up to car windows in some sections of the city. Newspaper also reported that estimates of rainfall across the county ranged from five inches in Baxter, where many roads were closed including the Elmore Town Ridge Bridge, to more than eight inches in other parts of the county.  Exactly what other parts of the county the article was referencing was not defined. A man was stranded in a vehicle in Darwin Park where three feet of water was reported. City Fire Crews rescued him in a short amount of time and was pronounced in good health. County Emergency management also responded to a call on Fisk Road, where neighbors were concerned about a women whose house was flooding. City Fire Crews got the resident out of the house quickly since water was approaching the heating and cooling units. Another area that experienced flooding was Byrne Avenue, where Eagle Creek Apartments had          flood waters reportedly reaching as high as two feet inside them, causing damage to furniture, school supplies, and musical instruments. Vehicles parked outside next to  these apartments were damaged.  Reports were received of nearly 20 roads closed or impassible due to high water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in areal flooding from Jacksboro to La Follette, Tennessee. Several area roads had several inches to nearly a foot of water over the roads in this vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center weakening as it continued to move to the east out of the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region, it continued to usher in rich southerly moisture across the region. Because of the proximity to the approaching low, mainly western portions of Middle Tennessee received flooding/flash flooding. One of the hardest hit areas was near Christiana in Rutherford County, where 50 homes were damaged due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains resulted in a few roads being closed across the county as the result of flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across mainly the western half of Middle Tennessee as the day progressed in response to deepening surface and aloft southerly flow. This was in association with a developing low pressure system over the central plains. As a result, these areas experienced flooding/flash flooding. One elderly man was killed indirectly by lightning in Marshall County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred in low lying areas around Clifton. Several roads around Clifton became impassible, including Main Street and West Elm Street which were closed due to the flooding. Newspaper also reported that that the Hasti-Mart convenience store was flooded. The store suffered no major damage, only a clean up was needed of 3-4 inches of water that covered the floor.
