EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure tracked across east Tennessee bringing damaging non-thunderstorm winds to the area late in the day on the 8th and continued into the overnight hours on the 9th. The strongest winds occurred over the higher elevations.  Flash flooding was also reported in the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson City Standard Banner newspaper reported a few trees and powerlines downed by non-thunderstorm winds across the county.  The wind also damaged a carport and severely damaged a roof of a mobile home in Dandridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers developed during the early morning hours across southwestern portions of Middle Tennessee in response to deepening southerly flow ushering in additional moisture to the area. The hardest hit location was Lawrenceburg in Lawrence County where flash flooding resulted in a person being trapped on top of a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported six inches of water over West Point Road in the city of Lawrenceburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout the day, flash flooding occurred mainly across western portions of Middle Tennessee. A large area of showers and thunderstorms moved across the area in association with southerly flow surface and aloft as an upper level trough approached the area. Flash flooding occurred across several counties, resulting in flooding of several area roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported as water was over Old Lebanon Dirt Road and North MT Juliet Road in MT Juliet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms continued to train over Hamilton county, Tennessee during the early morning hours of September 17th. Much of the county had extensive flash flooding that eventually evolved into an areal flood event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in numerous roads being closed city wide. Four feet of water was reported in the East Ridge area. Several homes and businesses, including an apartment complex just south of the Red Bank area had several inches of water moving into the apartments and the complex had to be evacuated due to the high flood water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms continued to slowly move across the area with flash flooding noted in three counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in several roads being closed due to flood waters rising at least a foot in height in several areas along highway 30 in the Athens, Tennessee area. Flooding continued across portions of the county for nearly 10 hours after the rain had ended across the area shortly before 10 pm on the 17th. Flood warnings were issued from 10 pm to 7 am to account for the continued left over flooding that resulted from previous rainfall during the evening of the 17th across the county.

These severe thunderstorms produced thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, heavy rainfall, and a tornado in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were snapped and uprooted.  Roof damage was reported to a trailer and a car wash.  Siding was peeled back off homes on Laws Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across mainly northern portions of the eastern half of Middle Tennessee, flooding/flash flooding occurred throughout the day. Both women were rescued, taken to local hospitals, and pronounced in good health.  CoCoRaHS observer near Bon Aqua reported 3.13 inches of rainfall associated with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center weakening as it continued to move to the east out of the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region, it continued to usher in rich southerly moisture across the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few public roads were covered with water, but several private roads and access lanes were impassible and many yards were turned into ponds across the county. The location receiving the greatest amount of rainfall was in Unionville, where a local CoCoRaHS observer reported 7.20 inches of rainfall. Other locations across the county ranged from 7 inches at Community High School to 2.85 inches at the Shelbyville Municipal Airport near Deason. One of the most hardest-hit locations was DJ's Market and Deli, on U.S. 231 North, across from Blackberry Ridge Golf Club, where up to five inches of water inundated the store. Part of the store's parking lot was also damaged, with large chunks cut into the pavement. Water also rose in several places along Liberty Pike (State Highway 269) north of Bell Buckle. The entrance to Parker Sain Road was impassible late in the morning and water was almost to the edge of the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a well developed surface and aloft low pressure center weakening as it continued to move to the east out of the Southern Mississippi River Valley Region, it continued to usher in rich southerly moisture across the region. Because of the proximity to the approaching low, mainly western portions of Middle Tennessee received flooding/flash flooding. One of the hardest hit areas was near Christiana in Rutherford County, where 50 homes were damaged due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the roads and flooding of at least 50 homes in Christiana area was reported. Type of water damage to homes was unknown. Newspaper also reported that eighteen families had to be evacuated in the county because of the persistent rain that had cows wading in the water almost up to their neck. Boats and trackers were used to rescue people stranded in their homes. No one was reported hurt. Newspaper also reported that Christiana Elementary School's gym was being used as a shelter for those being evacuated. CoCoRaHS reports from this area showed up to 6 inches of rainfall having occurred. In the Christiana community, waters reportedly rose to more than 8 feet deep at one point. Cars, sheds, and homes across certain parts of the Christiana area were submerged. The Stones River swelled to a larger size than one local resident said she has seen in her entire 50 years of living in Rutherford County. 23 roads were closed across the county.  A few miles away|from Long Creek on Christiana-Hover's Gap Road, a local farmer had 60 hay bales|moved to this location. These hay bales were picked up by moving flood waters|and crashed through a high tensile fence held up by steel pools. These bales had|to be moved back to Hover's Gap Road by six individuals. Each of these bales weighed 1,200 pounds apiece when they are dry, retail for $80 each, with a lot of them getting water logged. Several pieces of farm equipment were also damaged by rising waters, which will require at least mechanical work. A nearby farm reportedly lost 400 chickens to the flood. This report could not be confirmed. A car was swept away by rising waters on Elam Road near Christiana. The driver was not injured. The fast moving flood waters receded across the area by late Thursday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms, supported by strong winds aloft and a surface cold front, moved through Middle Tennessee across the mid state on Friday, October 9th. Along with several reports of thunderstorm wind damage, a funnel cloud citing, a high wind event causing damage to several homes and the downing of several trees, and a heavy rain event that was a factor in a multiple car accident, an EF-1 tornado touchdown in Robertson County and lifted in Davidson County. This was the first tornado in October ever recorded for these two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were in critical conditions following a three-vehicle wreck at the intersection of Woodbury Highway and Riddle Road in Manchester. A total of 10 persons were transported by ambulance from the wreck site to local hospitals. People involved in the accident ranged from age four to age 68. An ambulance official reported that extremely heavy rainfall conditions were likely a factor in the wreck. Final conditions of the 10 individuals, including two that were in critical condition, was unknown. This three vehicle accident included a 2004 Toyota Sierra Van that carried six people, 2007 Toyota Highlander that carried three people, and a 2003 Red Durango that carried a single person. Exact total damage amounts to the vehicles was unknown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds caused by a cold front moving through southern areas of the county blew down at least a couple dozen trees, blocked roads, and removed roofs from home residences. Amount of home residences roofs removed was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited severe convection developed at mid evening across the Cumberland Plateau in the vicinity of a slow moving frontal boundary. Deep moisture and lift in this weather regime featuring the passage of several upper level systems near and in advance of the boundary resulted in copious rainfall across the Cumberland Plateau and subsequent flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on East Valley Road three and a half miles south of Center Point.

Along with several reports of thunderstorm wind damage, a funnel cloud citing, a high wind event causing damage to several homes and the downing of several trees, and a heavy rain event that was a factor in a multiple car accident, an EF-1 tornado touchdown in Robertson County and lifted in Davidson County. This was the first tornado in October ever recorded for these two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of several trees down with damage to trailers with possible injuries. Type of trailers and extent of any possible injuries was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to drop into the mid to upper 20s, causing widespread freezing conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold area of high pressure and clear skies allowed temperatures to drop to around 30 degrees across Franklin county. The temperatures was 32 degrees or lower for at least 3 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms, supported by strong winds aloft and a surface cold front, moved through Middle Tennessee across the mid state on Friday, October 9th. Along with several reports of thunderstorm wind damage, a funnel cloud citing, a high wind event causing damage to several homes and the downing of several trees, and a heavy rain event that was a factor in a multiple car accident, an EF-1 tornado touchdown in Robertson County and lifted in Davidson County. This was the first tornado in October ever recorded for these two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down on a farm near the intersection of Greer Road and Dickson Lane in Robertson County. The tornado then continued on a 1.82 mile path east-northeast into Davidson County. The last evidence of damage was at a residence along Baker Road. An old barn was destroyed near the beginning of the damage path in Robertson County. The most significant damage was right along Greer Road, which marks the border between Robertson and Davidson Counties. On the Robertson County side of the road, dozens of hardwood trees were snapped or uprooted and a residence and outbuilding received roof damage. On the Davidson County side, several trailers were damaged, including one which was pushed approximately 15 feet of it's foundation. A woman sustained minor injuries in one of the trailers.  This was the first recorded October tornado in either Robertson or Davidson Counties.

Several counties received thunderstorm wind damage, mainly in the form of downed trees and building damage, and one person was struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in McEwen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South on Independence Day 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 4th. The storms continued into the morning hours of July 5th before dissipating. A few of these storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. Storms redeveloped by the afternoon of July 5th across West Tennessee in the vicinity of the cold front. These storms produced additional flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down several trees across northern portions of Lauderdale County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large 50 to 60 foot oak tree crushed two trailer homes on West 12th Street, injuring one women and trapping a couple who had to wait for firefighters to rescue them. The one women sustained an ankle injury and the couple was not injured. It was reported to have just blown down, broke off right at the ground, and hit two trailers, smashing them all the way down to their frames.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree that fell on a 66 year old women, who lives about five miles southwest from Crossville on Lantana Road, as thunderstorms moved through this area.  She was rushed to a regional trauma center after she was rescued. She was trapped underneath the tree for some period of time, but was conscious during the entire event. Extent of injury and condition were not reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South during the afternoon hours of July 30th, 2009. A low pressure center developed along the front as the front moved into the area. The low pressure helped to spawn several tornadoes across the Mid-South. In addition, other storms produced damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding into the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just east of the intersection of Interstate 40 and Appling Road. The tornado damaged a couple of baseball fields and proceeded northeast. A Kohl's department store and a Toyota dealership sustained damage. Several vehicles had windows blown out. A tractor-trailer was also overturned in the Kohl's parking lot. The tornado then crossed Germantown Parkway and damaged a Shogun Japanese Restaurant, a gas station and several other businesses. The tornado then proceeded northeast into a residential neighborhood where 97 homes sustained minor damage. Four apartment complexes and a total of 25 businesses received at least minor damage. Trees were also uprooted and snapped in the area. The tornado then moved into a golf course where at least 50 trees, fences and one small building was damaged. The tornado lifted near the center of the golf course. An off-duty firefighter was killed a few days later cleaning up storm debris when a tree fell on him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South during the afternoon hours of July 30th, 2009. A low pressure center developed along the front as the front moved into the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding around Oakland. Several homes in the North Woods Subdivision were flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across the Mid-South during the day on June 12th, 2009. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system developed over Eastern Oklahoma and tracked east into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours. A derecho tracked across the Mid-South producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. Wind speeds were reported as high as 80 mph. Isolated tornadoes occurred along the bow echo. In addition, scattered thunderstorms developed over ahead of the bow echo and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near the intersection of Bartlett Boulevard and Elmore Road in Bartlett. This tornado snapped numerous hardwood trees and caused minor roof damage to several homes. The estimated maximum winds were 105 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of the roof was removed causing significant damage inside the Dale's Superstyles Building in Centerville. This is a three story building, with an apartment on the third level. A piece of flashing ended up on the north side of Centerville Square, which is next to this building. It was also raining at this time, with water raining down into a bedroom, two bathrooms and a hallway, as well as on the ceiling tile. It also damaged the back room and the ceiling of the second floor hair salon. Water damage reached as far as the basement of the building, where a local business operates. Winds were estimated as high as 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low remained near the region from September 22 through September 26. Several rounds of thunderstorms develop over the area producing flash flooding over parts of southwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in the Bemis area after nearly 2.5 inches of rain fell in about 90 minutes. At least one car stalled out because of the excessive water on the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low remained near the region from September 22 through September 26. Several rounds of thunderstorms develop over the area producing flash flooding over parts of southwest Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one road in southeast McNairy county was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across east Tennessee brought windy conditions over the mountains.  Three people in a car were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 63 knots (72 mph) at the NOAA wind tower at Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient across east Tennessee brought windy conditions over the mountains.  Three people in a car were injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted to 61 knots (71 mph) at the Cove Mountain fire tower west of Gatlinburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 14th-September 16th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Midtown Memphis. Several streets were flooded as a result including Central Avenue at Fenwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 14th-September 16th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Mercer. Several roads were flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 14th-September 16th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Hornsby. A ball field along Highway 64 east of town had at least one foot of standing water as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A highly organized mesoscale convective vorticity maximum with strong low to mid tropospheric flow coupled with moderate instability resulted in the development of discrete supercellular thunderstorms. These storms produced a long-lived tornado across Northeast Tennessee late in the afternoon and another long duration tornado across Southwest Virginia later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a tornado on the ground moving east toward Thorn Hill. The tornado touched down 5.2 miles northeast of Thorn Hill in Grainger county then crossed into Hancock county. Wind speed was estimated at 80 mph in Grainger county at EF0 intensity.  Damage included the downing and uprooting of numerous trees in Grainger county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across the area during the afternoon hours triggering scattered severe thunderstorms over east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.   Four EF0 tornadoes develop along the front along with numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down about 2 miles east of Englewood.  The path length was two-tenths of a mile and path width was 30 yards.  The maximum wind speed was 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of showers and thunderstorms covered most of Middle Tennessee during the early afternoon through the mid evening hours as a strong cold front moved from the Ohio Valley into Middle Tennessee, resulting in two EF1 Tornadoes, heavy rainfall, flash flooding, large hail, trees downed, and some structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Shubert.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers developed during the early morning hours across southwestern portions of Middle Tennessee in response to deepening southerly flow ushering in additional moisture to the area. The hardest hit location was Lawrenceburg in Lawrence County where flash flooding resulted in a person being trapped on top of a car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported person trapped on top of car on Long Branch Road southwest of the city of Lawrenceburg. Final condition of person and car was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a frontal system moved northeastward from Middle Tennessee into the Ohio River Valley, the atmosphere became increasingly unstable, and with the influences of daytime heating and passages of upper level disturbances, thunderstorms developed from the early afternoon and lasted well into the evening hours. Several thunderstorms produced damaging thunderstorm winds while two reports of lightning damage occurred also.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed across State Highway 48 and State Highway 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed on the Cumberland Plateau as low level moisture was abundantly supplied to the region on strengthening southerly winds. Instability resulted from a steady flow of cold air aloft in association with a strong upper level trough over the mid south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down on East Valley Road two miles east of Dunlap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved from the Ohio Valley during the early morning hours into Middle Tennessee during the mid afternoon hours, an unstable airmass was already in place across much of the Southeastern U.S. This resulted in development across much of the mid state from the morning through late evening hours of severe thunderstorms, which produced large hail and thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down across Randolph Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front that was moving east to west across Middle Tennessee transitioned into a cold front by the afternoon hours west of the Tennessee River, and upper level disturbances developed that moved into the mid state from the west, Middle Tennessee was situated for the development of severe thunderstorms as a warm and unstable airmass took hold. Severe thunderstorms did develop during the late morning through late evening hours across mainly northern portions of the mid state, resulting in several thunderstorm wind damage and one hail report being received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported downed in the Clark Mountain Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the Mid-South on Independence Day 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed out ahead of the front during the late afternoon and early evening hours on the 4th. The storms continued into the morning hours of July 5th before dissipating. A few of these storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding. Storms redeveloped by the afternoon of July 5th across West Tennessee in the vicinity of the cold front. These storms produced additional flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding over several low water crossings in Decatur County. Bible Hill Road, Maxwell Street, and Concord Road were flooded as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across the area brought scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours in east Tennessee.  Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported a few trees downed by thunderstorm winds near Harriman Highway northeast of Harriman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Mid-South during the early morning hours of July 26th, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front. The showers and storms continued into the morning hours before dying out. Some of the storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a garage to catch fire. The garage sustained damage from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As upper level disturbances rotated around the eastern periphery of an upper level trough positioned across the mid state, and southwesterly low level flow ushered in deep low level moisture, severe thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon through evening hours, mainly across the eastern half of Middle Tennessee. These severe thunderstorms produced thunderstorm wind damage, large hail, heavy rainfall, and a tornado in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Resident on County Highway 1757 reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed along a cold front and moved into the Mid-South during the evening of August 4th, 2009. The remnants of the complex moved southeast over the area into the early morning hours of August 5th, 2009. Some thunderstorms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew several trees and power lines down across Northern and Eastern Weakley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered convection formed mainly north of Interstate 40 across Northeast Tennessee.  Storm formation was initiated by an upper level trough of low pressure crossing the Southern Appalachians in a weakly to moderately unstable air mass in place across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down at Flint Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm complex tracked along a boundary during the afternoon and evening hours.   Most of the storm reports were for thunderstorm wind damage, but a few hail occurrences were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported one tree downed by thunderstorm winds northeast of Huntsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed along a boundary and tracked across east Tennessee bringing numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 mph wind gust measured at the TVA tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produce flash flooding around the Northern and Eastern portions of Shelby County. Numerous roads were closed as a result including Brunswick Road, the intersection of Walnut Grove Road and Rock Creek, the intersection of Humphrey and Houston Levee Roads, and Macon Road east of Pisgah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved northward as a warm front during the daytime on December 8th, 2009. As the front moved north, an area of low pressure developed along the front. The low pressure brought several periods of heavy rain to West Tennessee. Flash flooding occurred as a result of the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding all around Decatur County. Numerous roads were closed due to high water including Pugh Road, Fairgrounds Road, and Gibson Road. In addition, Maxwell Street in Parsons was closed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse convection developed ahead of a frontal boundary which was moving slowly south across the Kentucky border into Northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down along Highway 25 W two miles north northwest of White Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought flooding rainfall between 2 and 5 inches across much of Lincoln county Tennessee.  This produced scattered areas of flash flooding, primarily south of Fayetteville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials closed the intersection of Highland Rim and Old Huntsville road due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm moving across the Mississipi Valley brought a dry line and cold front into north Alabama during the afternoon and early evening hours.  Several classic supercell thunderstorms developed by early afternoon in northwest Alabama, sweeping across the remainder of north Alabama during mid to late afternoon hours.  Many of the storms produced very large hail, up to baseball and softball sized, producing significant damage, especially from Decatur through Madison and northwest Huntsville.   One of the supercells produced a long track tornado producing damage of up to EF-3 intensity which struck northeastern Marshall County, crossed Lake Guntersville, and moved into southern DeKalb County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was knocked down on a house on Oak Hills Drive. Nearby on Cobb Hollows Drive two trees were blown down. One on a second house and another on a car. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed over Oklahoma during the early morning hours of June 15th, 2009. The complex moved east along a stationary front eventually moving into the Mid-South during the day continuing into the early evening hours on June 15th, 2009. Many storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph just west of Mercer on Highway 138.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across the area during the afternoon hours triggering scattered severe thunderstorms over east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.   Four EF0 tornadoes develop along the front along with numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported one tree downed by thunderstorm winds on Mahoney Road southeast of Oliver Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a warm front that was moving east to west across Middle Tennessee transitioned into a cold front by the afternoon hours west of the Tennessee River, and upper level disturbances developed that moved into the mid state from the west, Middle Tennessee was situated for the development of severe thunderstorms as a warm and unstable airmass took hold. Severe thunderstorms did develop during the late morning through late evening hours across mainly northern portions of the mid state, resulting in several thunderstorm wind damage and one hail report being received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds assoicated with a thunderstorm downed a large tree at the farm of Terry and Saranne Winfield, which landed on top of two pickup trucks, completely destroying them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system shifted slowly southeast across Eastern Kentucky which resulted in a weak to moderately unstable atmosphere over Eastern Tennessee for the third day in a row. Most of the convection was confined to Northeast and Central East Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down along Asheville Highway ten miles south of Greeneville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low level  meso-scale convective vorticity maximum over middle Tennessee created forcing for ascent.  Pulse convection developed in a region of moderate instability and weak deep layer shear affecting Southeast Tennessee and Southwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the vicinity of Midtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited convection developed across Central East and Southeast Tennessee in weak to moderate shear and instability environment at peak heating. Some flash flooding occurred due to slow storm movement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was occurring with a large section of highway 58 flooded and under water with around two feet of water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms,ahead of an approaching surface front and upper level trough, moved west to east from the southwest during the early afternoon hours through the late evening hours. Some of these thunderstorms were severe, and caused a tornado in Davidson County and flash flooding across mainly western portions of Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on U.S. Highway 64.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a frontal system moved northeastward from Middle Tennessee into the Ohio River Valley, the atmosphere became increasingly unstable, and with the influences of daytime heating and passages of upper level disturbances, thunderstorms developed from the early afternoon and lasted well into the evening hours. Several thunderstorms produced damaging thunderstorm winds while two reports of lightning damage occurred also.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in Pleasant View along Old Clarksville Pike and Oaklawn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast out of the Southern Plains during May 8th into the early morning of May 9th, 2009. A MCS developed in association with the low and tracked across Northeast Arkansas, the Missouri Bootheel and Northwest Tennessee. Storms associated with the MCS produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding.
A thunderstorm complex developed along a boundary and tracked across east Tennessee bringing numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.   A few hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported several trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds in Athens and the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two thunderstorm complexes moved out of southern Kentucky into Middle Tennessee as a result of strong northwesterly flow aloft. The first one moved across the Cumberland Plateau Region during the afternoon hours, while the second one moved across western and central portions of the mid state during the evening hours. Several counties received thunderstorm wind damage, mainly in the form of downed trees and building damage, and one person was struck by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof was blown off furniture store in downtown Dover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms from an earlier mesoscale convective system that developed over Southeast Kansas and Southwest Missouri moved east-southeast and affected northern parts of the Mid-South. Strong wind shear combined with very unstable air to support storms with damaging winds along and just ahead of a squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 to 70 mph in Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across the Mid-South during the day on June 12th, 2009. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system developed over Eastern Oklahoma and tracked east into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours. A derecho tracked across the Mid-South producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. Wind speeds were reported as high as 80 mph. Isolated tornadoes occurred along the bow echo. In addition, scattered thunderstorms developed over ahead of the bow echo and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew numerous trees down in Bartlett. Trees fell on houses in the vicinity of Sycamore View Road and Interstate 240. Power lines were knocked down at Yale Road near Covington Pike. Windows were shattered from a building on Kirby-Whitten Road just south of Summer Avenue. The front of the building was destroyed. A front porch was also damaged in the area. Trees were knocked down on Memphis-Arlington Road and Highway 70. Several dozen trees were knocked down on Sycamore View Road and Bartlett Road. Ellendale was also hit hard as numerous old trees were knocked down in the neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across the Mid-South during the day on June 12th, 2009. During the early morning hours, a mesoscale convective system developed over Eastern Oklahoma and tracked east into the Mid-South during the afternoon and evening hours. A derecho tracked across the Mid-South producing numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail and flash flooding. Wind speeds were reported as high as 80 mph. Isolated tornadoes occurred along the bow echo. In addition, scattered thunderstorms developed over ahead of the bow echo and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell at the intersection of Forest Hill-Irene Road and Poplar Avenue in Germantown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front across the Tennessee valley region continued to aid in the development of very heavy rainfall that contributed to flash flooding that evolved into a large areal flood event across southeast through northeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall resulted in areal flooding in the Decatur, Tennessee area, with several inches to around a foot of water reported to be over highway 58.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across east Tennessee triggered scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  Most of the storm reports were for damaging thunderstorm winds, but hail as large as quarters was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department personnel reported three trees downed by thunderstorm winds near Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front approached and moved through Middle Tennessee during the morning through late evening hours, the atmosphere became increasingly unstable. With the aid of daytime heating and passage of upper level disturbances, thunderstorms developed across the mid state as a result. Some of these thunderstorms produced damaging thunderstorm winds, heavy rainfall resulting in flash flooding, and lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into Middle Tennessee during the early morning hours, the atmosphere continued along and ahead of the front to remain rich in moisture. With instability in the atmosphere still present, and lift being provided by approaching frontal dynamics, thunderstorms developed that produced heavy rainfall resulting in flash flooding across four counties in the mid state through the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several areas across Southern Benton County were flooded. Water washed away the road at the intersection of Camp Mack Morris and Birdsong Road. Road crews worked through the night and into the morning hours of Friday, July 17th getting several of the roads back open. It is estimated that southern areas received up to 9 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Middle Tennessee producing several severe thunderstorms that caused wind damage to many locations, produced hail, a funnel cloud, and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Tennessee National Wildlife Refuge Duck River Unit, a 50 kt wind gust was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across east Tennessee triggered scattered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon hours.  Most of the storm reports were for damaging thunderstorm winds, but hail as large as quarters was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highway department reported penny-size hail fell near Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed along a boundary and tracked across east Tennessee bringing numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 63mph was measured at a ORNL tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well organized damaging comma head/bow echo system with strong shear and weak to moderate instability plowed into the Southern Appalachian region producing predominantly severe wind gusts with limited reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported in Huntsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked across east Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours.  The resulting squall line triggered numerous thunderstorm wind damage reports along with large hail.  Three tornadoes were also reported.  One person was injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The newspaper reported a several trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds in Graysville.  In addition, a few homes sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited severe convection developed at mid evening across the Cumberland Plateau in the vicinity of a slow moving frontal boundary. Deep moisture and lift in this weather regime featuring the passage of several upper level systems near and in advance of the boundary resulted in copious rainfall across the Cumberland Plateau and subsequent flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Buck Ridge Road three and a half miles northeast of Lewis Chapel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered multi-cell convection developed in weak to moderately unstable atmospheric conditions across Southeast Tennessee producing isolated down bursts and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down near Cleveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front continued to affect the Midsouth during the day on May 16th, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
